authoritative_sources.title: Safety and Health Regulations for Construction
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator and Part 1926 is the controlling federal baseline for construction safety obligations and hazard-control boundaries. It is authoritative for deciding when dispatch coordination must stop and escalate due to safety conditions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: site-safety gating and refusal or escalation boundaries
used_for: refusal of safety-signoff or execution authority

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Process
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City School Construction Authority
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC SCA publishes a detailed public-owner construction workflow describing closeout, inspections, schedules, site safety plans, permits, change orders, and subcontractor approvals for active construction work. It is authoritative for workflow-stage, artifact, and handoff expectations in a real public construction environment.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: dispatch intake, schedule control, permits, inspections, and closeout
used_for: site safety plan and permit responsibilities

authoritative_sources.title: Obtain a Certificate of Occupancy
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is the authoritative local permitting and occupancy regulator. This guidance defines legal occupancy, final approval, temporary occupancy, unresolved-issue handling, and agency sign-off requirements that materially gate closeout and turnover decisions.

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Manual Section 3-5 Control of Work
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Caltrans is a major public owner with published construction operations standards. This section defines order-of-work, revised progress schedules, contractor assignments, and subcontracting requests, making it authoritative for schedule sequencing, owner verification, and subcontractor-related dispatch controls.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: scheduling, owner assignment, and subcontractor control

authoritative_sources.title: Contractor requirements, certifications, and qualifications for fire alarm and water-based fire suppression
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA is a major federal public owner and facilities operator. This standard is authoritative for work-order content, critical versus non-critical response timing, testing-after-repair, written certification, and full repair-record expectations in field-service dispatch workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: work-order intake, response timing, testing, and completion evidence

workflow_stages.name: Dispatch intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request scope, work-order intent, owner chain, site conditions, permit status, and safety state before any booking or route commitment is made.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Dependency mapping and system-of-record confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Build the dependency chain across field-service, project-management, work-order, permitting, inspection, and safety systems to determine whether work can proceed, be blocked, or be rerouted.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Cross-team routing and owner resolution
workflow_stages.description: Identify prior owner, next owner, subcontractor readiness, schedule conflicts, and acceptance state without absorbing execution authority.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Safety, permit, and inspection escalation handling
workflow_stages.description: Stop routing when hazard controls, permits, inspections, or code-triggered sign-offs change whether work may proceed and escalate to the correct authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Closeout and closure confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Issue closure only when destination owner, inspection outcomes, occupancy or turnover conditions, and required closeout artifacts are explicit and complete.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: work orders, service tickets, and dispatch or booking requests
permit applications, permit status, inspection requests, and inspection results
site logs, site safety plans, safety observations, and test or witness records
subcontractor approvals, subcontract scopes, and start or end date records
change orders, progress schedules, punch lists, and deficiency logs
warranties, O&M manuals, training acknowledgements, occupancy records, and closeout packages
systems_of_record: document repository for closeout, photos, and as-builts

allowed: Classify dispatch requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Track dependencies, duplicated ownership, and blocker state across construction and field-service systems.
Produce handoff summaries, dependency trackers, routing confirmations, and closure confirmations when evidence is complete.
escalate: Required site, permit, inspection, safety, schedule, subcontractor, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
The request requires permit approval, code interpretation, engineering judgement, licensed trade signoff, financial approval, or safety-critical authority.
A public baseline and tenant or jurisdiction policy materially conflict on permit, occupancy, inspection, safety, or closeout handling.
A safety deficiency, failed test, or inspection requirement changes whether the work may proceed.
refuse: Book or close work without destination owner, required artifacts, or blocker resolution.
Approve permits, code compliance, occupancy, or licensed trade work without explicit authority.
Direct crew execution, scope approval, or system-of-record completion updates as if the lane owned execution.

domain_failure_modes: work is handed off without permit status, site-readiness evidence, or access prerequisites
multiple teams claim dispatch or field ownership and the wrong crew or trade is committed
inspection, special-inspection, or test failures are ignored and work proceeds anyway
closeout is treated as complete without punch-list resolution, O&M manuals, warranties, or occupancy documentation
the lane is pressured into code interpretation, permit approval, licensed trade signoff, or field execution
subcontractor scope or approval state is unresolved but the work is scheduled anyway
tenant-specific permit or closeout policies differ from the public baseline and the lane guesses instead of escalating

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit closure, temporary occupancy, and special-inspection sequencing rules differ materially across cities, owners, and project types.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline defines the need for permit, inspection, and occupancy evidence but does not eliminate local variation. Runtime must retrieve jurisdiction, owner contract requirements, and tenant escalation policy before asserting project-specific release posture.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, safety, and failure handling instead of generic appointment scheduling only.
The lane stops at coordination. Permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, engineering judgement, and field execution remain outside the lane even when schedule pressure is high.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because all known jurisdictional variation is handled through explicit runtime retrieval and escalation.
